AI.method: The Yates covariance decomposition of the Brier score is algebraically rearranged into three distinct non-negative components: variance mismatch, correlation deficit, and calibration-in-the-large.
AI.result: The approach clarifies conditions for perfect forecasting: matching outcome variance, achieving perfect correlation with outcomes, and aligning with the mean of outcomes.
AI.conclusion: Any deviation from these optimality criteria directly contributes to an increased Brier score, reinforcing the importance of addressing these factors for forecast improvement.

title: Text-Driven Emotionally Continuous Talking Face Generation
summary: Talking Face Generation (TFG) strives to create realistic and emotionally expressive digital faces. While previous TFG works have mastered the creation of naturalistic facial movements, they typically express a fixed target emotion in synthetic videos and lack the ability to exhibit continuously changing and natural expressions like humans do when conveying information. To synthesize realistic videos, we propose a novel task called Emotionally Continuous Talking Face Generation (EC-TFG), which takes a text segment and an emotion description with varying emotions as driving data, aiming to generate a video where the person speaks the text while reflecting the emotional changes within the description. Alongside this, we introduce a customized model, i.e., Temporal-Intensive Emotion Modulated Talking Face Generation (TIE-TFG), which innovatively manages dynamic emotional variations by employing Temporal-Intensive Emotion Fluctuation Modeling, allowing it to provide emotion variation sequences corresponding to the input text to drive continuous facial expression changes in synthesized videos. Extensive evaluations demonstrate our method's exceptional ability to produce smooth emotion transitions and uphold high-quality visuals and motion authenticity across diverse emotional states.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces "Emotionally Continuous Talking Face Generation" (EC-TFG), a method for generating videos of talking faces with smoothly changing emotions based on input text and emotion descriptions.
AI.motivation: Existing works in Talking Face Generation lack the ability to depict continuously shifting human-like emotional expressions, essential for creating realistic and expressive synthetic videos.
AI.method: The authors propose a novel task, EC-TFG, alongside a customized model called TIE-TFG, which uses Temporal-Intensive Emotion Fluctuation Modeling to generate videos with smooth emotional transitions based on input text and emotion descriptions.
AI.result: The evaluations show that the method produces high-quality videos with dynamic, smooth emotion transitions and authenticity in motion and visuals across different emotions.
AI.conclusion: This approach sets a new benchmark for generating synthetic talking faces with dynamic and realistic emotional expressions, addressing a key limitation in previous TFG methods.

title: Lyapunov Probes for Hallucination Detection in Large Foundation Models
summary: We address hallucination detection in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) by framing the problem through the lens of dynamical systems stability theory. Rather than treating hallucination as a straightforward classification task, we conceptualize (M)LLMs as dynamical systems, where factual knowledge is represented by stable equilibrium points within the representation space. Our main insight is that hallucinations tend to arise at the boundaries of knowledge-transition regions separating stable and unstable zones. To capture this phenomenon, we propose Lyapunov Probes: lightweight networks trained with derivative-based stability constraints that enforce a monotonic decay in confidence under input perturbations. By performing systematic perturbation analysis and applying a two-stage training process, these probes reliably distinguish between stable factual regions and unstable, hallucination-prone regions. Experiments on diverse datasets and models demonstrate consistent improvements over existing baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an innovative approach to detect hallucinations in Large Language Models (LLMs) and Multimodal LLMs (MLLMs) using stability theory and Lyapunov Probes.
AI.motivation: The research aims to tackle the challenge of hallucination detection in LLMs and MLLMs, which is crucial for improving their reliability and performance.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize LLMs as dynamical systems, introducing Lyapunov Probes trained with derivative-based stability constraints to identify hallucination-prone regions.
AI.result: The experiments show that Lyapunov Probes reliably distinguish between stable factual zones and unstable regions associated with hallucinations, improving detection performance across datasets and models.
AI.conclusion: Lyapunov Probes demonstrate the ability to enhance hallucination detection by leveraging dynamical systems theory, offering a promising direction for more robust LLM and MLLM development.

title: Machine Learning for analysis of Multiple Sclerosis cross-tissue bulk and single-cell transcriptomics data
summary: Multiple Sclerosis (MS) is a chronic autoimmune disease of the central nervous system whose molecular mechanisms remain incompletely understood. In this study, we developed an end-to-end machine learning pipeline to analyze transcriptomic data from peripheral blood mononuclear cells and cerebrospinal fluid, integrating both bulk microarray and single-cell RNA sequencing datasets (concentrating on CD4+ and B-cells). After rigorous preprocessing, batch correction, and gene declustering, XGBoost classifiers were trained to distinguish MS patients from healthy controls. Explainable AI tools, namely SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP), were employed to identify key genes driving classification, and results were compared with Differential Expression Analysis (DEA). SHAP-prioritized genes were further investigated through interaction networks and pathway enrichment analyses. The models achieved strong performance, particularly in CSF B-cells (AUC=0.94) and microarray (AUC=0.86). SHAP gene selection proved to be complementary to classical DEA. Gene clusters identified across multiple datasets highlighted immune activation, non-canonical immune checkpoints (ITK, CLEC2D, KLRG1, CEACAM1), ribosomal and translational programs, ubiquitin-proteasome regulation, lipid trafficking, and Epstein-Barr virus-related pathways. Our integrative and explainable framework reveals complementary insights beyond conventional analysis and provides novel mechanistic hypotheses and potential biomarkers for MS pathogenesis.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a machine learning framework to identify genes involved in Multiple Sclerosis (MS) using transcriptomic datasets, achieving high performance and highlighting novel biomarkers.
AI.motivation: To better understand the molecular mechanisms of Multiple Sclerosis and identify potential biomarkers for its pathogenesis.
AI.method: An end-to-end pipeline was developed integrating bulk microarray and single-cell RNA sequencing data, with XGBoost classifiers and SHAP explainability tools to prioritize key genes.
AI.result: The model performed well (AUC up to 0.94), identified important immune-related genes and pathways, and showed SHAP's complementarity to Differential Expression Analysis.
AI.conclusion: The framework offers novel biological insights and biomarkers for MS, enhancing traditional analysis methods through an explainable approach.

title: FedARKS: Federated Aggregation via Robust and Discriminative Knowledge Selection and Integration for Person Re-identification
summary: The application of federated domain generalization in person re-identification (FedDG-ReID) aims to enhance the model's generalization ability in unseen domains while protecting client data privacy. However, existing mainstream methods typically rely on global feature representations and simple averaging operations for model aggregation, leading to two limitations in domain generalization: (1) Using only global features makes it difficult to capture subtle, domain-invariant local details (such as accessories or textures); (2) Uniform parameter averaging treats all clients as equivalent, ignoring their differences in robust feature extraction capabilities, thereby diluting the contributions of high quality clients. To address these issues, we propose a novel federated learning framework, Federated Aggregation via Robust and Discriminative Knowledge Selection and Integration (FedARKS), comprising two mechanisms: RK (Robust Knowledge) and KS (Knowledge Selection).

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Federated Aggregation via Robust and Discriminative Knowledge Selection and Integration (FedARKS) to improve person re-identification in unseen domains while preserving data privacy.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to overcome limitations in federated domain generalization for person re-identification caused by global-only feature reliance and uniform model aggregation.
AI.method: The proposed method, FedARKS, includes two mechanisms: RK (Robust Knowledge) to capture subtle domain-invariant local details, and KS (Knowledge Selection) to account for differences in clients' feature extraction capabilities.
AI.result: FedARKS enhances model aggregation in federated learning, leading to better generalization to unseen domains while respecting client privacy.
AI.conclusion: FedARKS is effective in improving federated domain generalization for person re-identification by addressing global feature and uniform aggregation issues.

title: Koopman Regularized Deep Speech Disentanglement for Speaker Verification
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Human speech contains both linguistic content and speaker dependent characteristics making speaker verification a key technology in identity critical applications. Modern deep learning speaker verification systems aim to learn speaker representations that are invariant to semantic content and nuisance factors such as ambient noise. However, many existing approaches depend on labelled data, textual supervision or large pretrained models as feature extractors, limiting scalability and practical deployment, raising sustainability concerns. We propose Deep Koopman Speech Disentanglement Autoencoder (DKSD-AE), a structured autoencoder that combines a novel multi-step Koopman operator learning module with instance normalization to disentangle speaker and content dynamics. Quantitative experiments across multiple datasets demonstrate that DKSD-AE achieves improved or competitive speaker verification performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines while maintaining high content EER, confirming effective disentanglement. These results are obtained with substantially fewer parameters and without textual supervision. Moreover, performance remains stable under increased evaluation scale, highlighting representation robustness and generalization. Our findings suggest that Koopman-based temporal modelling, when combined with instance normalization, provides an efficient and principled solution for speaker-focused representation learning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a new approach called Deep Koopman Speech Disentanglement Autoencoder (DKSD-AE) for speaker verification that is scalable, efficient, and does not require textual supervision or large pretrained models. It achieves competitive results while using fewer resources.
AI.motivation: To address limitations in existing speaker verification systems, such as heavy reliance on labeled data, textual supervision, and large pretrained models, which hinder scalability, sustainability, and practical deployment.
AI.method: DKSD-AE, an autoencoder combined with a multi-step Koopman operator learning module and instance normalization, is used to disentangle speaker and content features in speech.
AI.result: DKSD-AE demonstrates improved or competitive performance for speaker verification across datasets, effective speaker-content disentanglement, fewer parameters, and robustness under scale.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Koopman-based temporal modeling approach offers an efficient and principled solution to speaker representation learning, with robustness and sustainability advantages.

title: Cross-Resolution Distribution Matching for Diffusion Distillation
summary: Diffusion distillation is central to accelerating image and video generation, yet existing methods are fundamentally limited by the denoising process, where step reduction has largely saturated. Partial timestep low-resolution generation can further accelerate inference, but it suffers noticeable quality degradation due to cross-resolution distribution gaps. We propose Cross-Resolution Distribution Matching Distillation (RMD), a novel distillation framework that bridges cross-resolution distribution gaps for high-fidelity, few-step multi-resolution cascaded inference. Specifically, RMD divides the timestep intervals for each resolution using logarithmic signal-to-noise ratio (logSNR) curves, and introduces logSNR-based mapping to compensate for resolution-induced shifts. Distribution matching is conducted along resolution trajectories to reduce the gap between low-resolution generator distributions and the teacher's high-resolution distribution. In addition, a predicted-noise re-injection mechanism is incorporated during upsampling to stabilize training and improve synthesis quality. Quantitative and qualitative results show that RMD preserves high-fidelity generation while accelerating inference across various backbones. Notably, RMD achieves up to 33.4X speedup on SDXL and 25.6X on Wan2.1-14B, while preserving high visual fidelity.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RMD, a new framework to accelerate high-quality image and video generation by bridging resolution gaps and improving inference speed.
AI.motivation: Current distillation methods face limitations due to resolution-induced quality degradation during acceleration. Bridging the distribution gap is crucial for maintaining fidelity in quick inference.
AI.method: RMD employs logarithmic signal-to-noise ratio (logSNR) mapping to divide timestep intervals, bridges low-to-high resolution distribution gaps using trajectories, and stabilizes synthesis via a predicted-noise re-injection mechanism.
AI.result: RMD significantly improves inference speed, achieving up to 33.4X speedup on SDXL and 25.6X on Wan2.1-14B while ensuring high-quality outputs.
AI.conclusion: RMD offers a scalable and efficient solution for high-fidelity, accelerated image and video generation, addressing critical limitations of existing methods.

title: Place-it-R1: Unlocking Environment-aware Reasoning Potential of MLLM for Video Object Insertion
summary: Modern video editing techniques have achieved high visual fidelity when inserting video objects. However, they focus on optimizing visual fidelity rather than physical causality, leading to edits that are physically inconsistent with their environment. In this work, we present Place-it-R$1$, an end-to-end framework for video object insertion that unlocks the environment-aware reasoning potential of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs). Our framework leverages the Chain-of-Thought (CoT) reasoning of MLLMs to orchestrate video diffusion, following a Think-then-Place paradigm. To bridge cognitive reasoning and generative execution, we introduce three key innovations: First, MLLM performs physical scene understanding and interaction reasoning, generating environment-aware chain-of-thought tokens and inferring valid insertion regions to explicitly guide the diffusion toward physically plausible insertion. Then, we introduce MLLM-guided Spatial Direct Preference Optimization (DPO), where diffusion outputs are fed back to the MLLM for scoring, enabling visual naturalness. During inference, the MLLM iteratively triggers refinement cycles and elicits adaptive adjustments from the diffusion model, forming a closed-loop that progressively enhances editing quality. Furthermore, we provide two user-selectable modes: a plausibility-oriented flexible mode that permits environment modifications (\eg, generating support structures) to enhance physical plausibility, and a fidelity-oriented standard mode that preserves scene integrity for maximum fidelity, offering users explicit control over the plausibility-fidelity trade-off. Extensive experiments demonstrate Place-it-R1 achieves physically-coherent video object insertion compared with state-of-the-art solutions and commercial models.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Place-it-R1, a framework leveraging Multimodal Large Language Models for environment-aware video object insertion, focusing on physical causality and visual fidelity.
AI.motivation: Current video editing methods optimize visual fidelity but neglect physical causality, resulting in edits that are inconsistent with the environment.
AI.method: The proposed framework utilizes Multimodal Large Language Models for Chain-of-Thought reasoning, introducing physical scene understanding, Spatial Direct Preference Optimization, and iterative refinement to ensure video object insertion is both visually natural and physically plausible.
AI.result: Extensive experiments confirm Place-it-R1's ability to achieve physically-coherent video object insertion, outperforming state-of-the-art and commercial solutions.
AI.conclusion: Place-it-R1 provides a robust and adaptive solution for video object insertion, balancing physical plausibility and visual fidelity, with user-selectable modes for tailored editing applications.

title: Spatial Colour Mixing Illusions as a Perception Stress Test for Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong benchmark results, yet can exhibit systematic perceptual weaknesses: structured, large changes to pixel values can cause confident yet nonsensical predictions, even when the underlying scene remains easily recognizable to humans. We study this gap using Spatial Colour Mixing, a programmatic family of colour distortions that overlays structured patterns (in both RGB and Ostwald colour systems) onto natural images. We introduce a framework of eight spatial colour mixing variants and evaluate nine VLMs across three model families on four datasets. Across models and datasets, accuracy degrades sharply with increasing distortion, and scaling the language model does not reliably mitigate the failure. In a human study with 61 participants on an animal recognition dataset, humans substantially outperform VLMs under the same distortions. Finally, we show that a simple human-inspired preprocessing step recovers a meaningful portion of performance for several distortion types, motivating perception-aware preprocessing and tool-use as practical strategies for improving VLM robustness.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how Vision-Language Models (VLMs) fail under specific color distortions, identifying significant weaknesses in their robustness and proposing a preprocessing solution inspired by human perception.
AI.motivation: The study is motivated by the observation that Vision-Language Models often fail on perceptual tasks when faced with structured pixel distortions, despite their impressive benchmark performance.
AI.method: The paper introduces Spatial Colour Mixing, a set of systematic color distortions applied to natural images, and evaluates nine VLMs using these distortions across various benchmarks. Additionally, it compares VLMs' performance with humans and incorporates a preprocessing step to boost model robustness.
AI.result: The study shows that accuracy in VLMs drops significantly with increasing distortion levels and even scaling the language model size does not mitigate the issue effectively. Humans consistently outperform VLMs under identical conditions.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that VLMs require more robust perception-aware systems and suggests that simple preprocessing strategies inspired by human perception could improve their performance in distorted scenarios.

title: Longitudinal NSCLC Treatment Progression via Multimodal Generative Models
summary: Predicting tumor evolution during radiotherapy is a clinically critical challenge, particularly when longitudinal changes are driven by both anatomy and treatment. In this work, we introduce a Virtual Treatment (VT) framework that formulates non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) progression as a dose-aware multimodal conditional image-to-image translation problem. Given a CT scan, baseline clinical variables, and a specified radiation dose increment, VT aims to synthesize plausible follow-up CT images reflecting treatment-induced anatomical changes. We evaluate the proposed framework on a longitudinal dataset of 222 stage III NSCLC patients, comprising 895 CT scans acquired during radiotherapy under irregular clinical schedules. The generative process is conditioned on delivered dose increments together with demographic and tumor-related clinical variables. Representative GAN-based and diffusion-based models are benchmarked across 2D and 2.5D configurations. Quantitative and qualitative results indicate that diffusion-based models benefit more consistently from multimodal, dose-aware conditioning and produce more stable and anatomically plausible tumor evolution trajectories than GAN-based baselines, supporting the potential of VT as a tool for in-silico treatment monitoring and adaptive radiotherapy research in NSCLC.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a Virtual Treatment (VT) framework to predict tumor evolution during radiotherapy for non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC), using dose-aware, multimodal image-to-image translation methods.
AI.motivation: Predicting tumor evolution during radiotherapy is crucial for clinical decision-making, as changes are influenced by both anatomy and treatment.
AI.method: A Virtual Treatment framework is introduced, leveraging dose-aware multimodal conditional image-to-image translation. The approach uses CT scans, patient variables, and radiation dosage to synthesize follow-up CT images. GAN-based and diffusion-based models are benchmarked.
AI.result: The study shows that diffusion-based models, conditioned on delivered dose increments and clinical variables, outperform GAN-based models in generating anatomically plausible tumor evolution trajectories.
AI.conclusion: The VT framework demonstrates potential as a tool for monitoring tumor evolution and aiding adaptive radiotherapy research in NSCLC patients.

title: VLM-RobustBench: A Comprehensive Benchmark for Robustness of Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-language models (VLMs) achieve strong performance on standard, high-quality datasets, but we still do not fully understand how they perform under real-world image distortions. We present VLM-RobustBench, a benchmark spanning 49 augmentation types across noise, blur, weather, digital, and geometric perturbations, evaluated under graded severities (low/mid/high) and binary transforms, yielding 133 corrupted settings. We evaluate VLMs from four families (Qwen, InternVL, Molmo, Gemma) on two complementary benchmarks: MMBench (visually grounded) and MMMU-Pro (reasoning-oriented). Our results reveal that visual severity is a weak predictor of difficulty: low-severity spatial perturbations often degrade performance more than visually severe photometric corruptions. In particular, low-severity glass_blur reduces MMBench accuracy by about 8 pp on average across models, while the largest drops arise from resampling and geometric distortions (e.g., upsample, elastic_transform), reaching up to 34 pp. Overall, our findings suggest current VLMs are semantically strong but spatially fragile, motivating the definition of novel robustness evaluation protocols and training regimes that emphasize resampling and geometric invariances.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces VLM-RobustBench to evaluate the robustness of vision-language models (VLMs) against 133 types of image distortions and perturbations. Results show VLMs handle semantic challenges well but struggle with spatial distortions.
AI.motivation: To understand the real-world performance of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) under various image corruptions and distortions, which are less studied compared to ideal dataset conditions.
AI.method: Developed VLM-RobustBench with 49 augmentation types and graded severities, spanning noise, blur, weather, digital, and geometric perturbations. Tested VLMs against two benchmarks: MMBench and MMMU-Pro.
AI.result: Findings show VLMs are robust semantically but fragile spatially, with geometric distortions causing the most performance degradation. Low-severity perturbations like glass_blur also significantly impact accuracy.
AI.conclusion: VLMs require improved robustness protocols, focusing on resampling and geometric invariances to handle distortions effectively in real-world scenarios.

title: Reflective Flow Sampling Enhancement
summary: The growing demand for text-to-image generation has led to rapid advances in generative modeling. Recently, text-to-image diffusion models trained with flow matching algorithms, such as FLUX, have achieved remarkable progress and emerged as strong alternatives to conventional diffusion models. At the same time, inference-time enhancement strategies have been shown to improve the generation quality and text-prompt alignment of text-to-image diffusion models. However, these techniques are mainly applicable to conventional diffusion models and usually fail to perform well on flow models. To bridge this gap, we propose Reflective Flow Sampling (RF-Sampling), a theoretically-grounded and training-free inference enhancement framework explicitly designed for flow models, especially for the CFG-distilled variants (i.e., models distilled from CFG guidance techniques), like FLUX. Departing from heuristic interpretations, we provide a formal derivation proving that RF-Sampling implicitly performs gradient ascent on the text-image alignment score. By leveraging a linear combination of textual representations and integrating them with flow inversion, RF-Sampling allows the model to explore noise spaces that are more consistent with the input prompt. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate that RF-Sampling consistently improves both generation quality and prompt alignment. Moreover, RF-Sampling is also the first inference enhancement method that can exhibit test-time scaling ability to some extent on FLUX.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces Reflective Flow Sampling (RF-Sampling), an inference enhancement strategy for text-to-image flow models, particularly CFG-distilled flow models like FLUX.
AI.motivation: Various inference enhancement methods predominantly focus on conventional diffusion models, while flow models like FLUX have limited inference-based improvements. The paper aims to address this gap.
AI.method: RF-Sampling is a training-free framework leveraging textual representations and flow inversion to implicitly perform gradient ascent, enhancing text-image alignment and generation quality.
AI.result: RF-Sampling consistently improves generation quality and text-image alignment in benchmarks, providing a scaling ability during inference for models such as FLUX.
AI.conclusion: The proposed RF-Sampling framework effectively bridges the gap in inference enhancements for flow-based text-to-image models, achieving significant improvement without retraining.

title: FreeOcc: Training-free Panoptic Occupancy Prediction via Foundation Models
summary: Semantic and panoptic occupancy prediction for road scene analysis provides a dense 3D representation of the ego vehicle's surroundings. Current camera-only approaches typically rely on costly dense 3D supervision or require training models on data from the target domain, limiting deployment in unseen environments. We propose FreeOcc, a training-free pipeline that leverages pretrained foundation models to recover both semantics and geometry from multi-view images. FreeOcc extracts per-view panoptic priors with a promptable foundation segmentation model and prompt-to-taxonomy rules, and reconstructs metric 3D points with a reconstruction foundation model. Depth- and confidence- aware filtering lifts reliable labels into 3D, which are fused over time and voxelized with a deterministic refinement stack. For panoptic occupancy, instances are recovered by fitting and merging robust current-view 3D box candidates, enabling instance-aware occupancy without any learned 3D model. On Occ3D-nuScenes, FreeOcc achieves 16.9 mIoU and 16.5 RayIoU train-free, on par with state-of-the-art weakly supervised methods. When employed as a pseudo-label generation pipeline for training downstream models, it achieves 21.1 RayIoU, surpassing the previous state-of-the-art weakly supervised baseline. Furthermore, FreeOcc sets new baselines for both train-free and weakly supervised panoptic occupancy prediction, achieving 3.1 RayPQ and 3.9 RayPQ, respectively. These results highlight foundation-model-driven perception as a practical route to training-free 3D scene understanding.

AI.tldr: FreeOcc is a training-free pipeline leveraging pretrained foundation models for semantic and panoptic occupancy prediction in road scene analysis, attaining competitive results without dense 3D supervision.
AI.motivation: Addressing the limitations of current camera-only approaches that require dense 3D supervision or training with target domain data, hindering deployment in unseen environments.
AI.method: FreeOcc employs pretrained foundation models to extract semantic and geometric information, utilizing promptable segmentation models, depth-aware filtering, and deterministic refinement processes for train-free 3D scene understanding.
AI.result: FreeOcc showed competitive performance, achieving 16.9 mIoU and 16.5 RayIoU for train-free methods and surpassing weakly supervised baselines with 21.1 RayIoU on pseudo-label training.
AI.conclusion: Foundation-model-driven perception offers a viable pathway for training-free and weakly supervised 3D scene comprehension in road environments.

title: Random Dot Product Graphs as Dynamical Systems: Limitations and Opportunities
summary:
Can we learn the differential equations governing the evolution of a temporal network? We investigate this within Random Dot Product Graphs (RDPGs), where each network snapshot is generated from latent positions evolving under unknown dynamics. We identify three fundamental obstructions: gauge freedom from rotational ambiguity in latent positions, realizability constraints from the manifold structure of the probability matrix, and trajectory recovery artifacts from spectral embedding.
  We develop a geometric framework based on principal fiber bundles that formalizes these obstructions. We characterize invisible dynamics as exactly the skew-symmetric generators, and show the realizable tangent space has dimension $nd - d(d-1)/2$. An holonomy dichotomy emerges: polynomial dynamics have commuting generators, stationary eigenvectors, and trivial holonomy, making gauge alignment purely statistical; Laplacian dynamics satisfy a non-commutativity criterion producing nontrivial holonomy, with curvature weighted by $1/(λ_ι+ λ_γ)$ linking gauge sensitivity to the spectral gap. In $d=2$ this yields full restricted holonomy $\mathrm{SO}(2)$; for $d \ge 3$ generic full $\mathrm{SO}(d)$ remains conjectural. Cram'er--Rao lower bounds reveal that the same spectral gap controlling curvature and injectivity simultaneously controls Fisher information, so geometric and statistical difficulty are inextricable.
  We prove an identifiability principle: symmetric dynamics cannot absorb skew-symmetric gauge contamination, so dynamics structure can resolve gauge ambiguity. We demonstrate this constructively with anchor-based alignment and a UDE pipeline recovering vector fields from noisy graph sequences. Yet finite-sample interactions between noise, gauge, and dynamics expressiveness remain beyond the asymptotic theory. We frame this gap as an open challenge.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of learning differential equations governing temporal network evolution, presenting a geometric framework to address key obstructions and demonstrating methods to recover dynamics, though gaps remain in finite-sample conditions.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether differential equations governing temporal network evolution can be learned, and to address challenges posed by ambiguities and constraints inherent in such settings.
AI.method: Developed a geometric framework based on principal fiber bundles to formalize challenges, characterized dynamic behaviors via mathematical properties, and proposed identifiability principles and constructive methods for recovery from noisy network data.
AI.result: Proposed methods enabled recovery of vector fields and structure from noisy graph sequences, highlighting key dynamics properties, but finite-sample challenges remain unresolved.
AI.conclusion: The framework ties geometric and statistical challenges, with demonstrated techniques for recovering dynamics; however, resolving finite-sample interactions remains an open challenge.

title: A Semi-Supervised Framework for Breast Ultrasound Segmentation with Training-Free Pseudo-Label Generation and Label Refinement
summary:
Semi-supervised learning (SSL) has emerged as a promising paradigm for breast ultrasound (BUS) image segmentation, but it often suffers from unstable pseudo labels under extremely limited annotations, leading to inaccurate supervision and degraded performance. Recent vision-language models (VLMs) provide a new opportunity for pseudo-label generation, yet their effectiveness on BUS images remains limited because domain-specific prompts are difficult to transfer.
  To address this issue, we propose a semi-supervised framework with training-free pseudo-label generation and label refinement. By leveraging simple appearance-based descriptions (e.g., dark oval), our method enables cross-domain structural transfer between natural and medical images, allowing VLMs to generate structurally consistent pseudo labels. These pseudo labels are used to warm up a static teacher that captures global structural priors of breast lesions. Combined with an exponential moving average teacher, we further introduce uncertainty entropy weighted fusion and adaptive uncertainty-guided reverse contrastive learning to improve boundary discrimination.
  Experiments on four BUS datasets demonstrate that our method achieves performance comparable to fully supervised models even with only 2.5% labeled data, significantly outperforming existing SSL approaches. Moreover, the proposed paradigm is readily extensible: for other imaging modalities or diseases, only a global appearance description is required to obtain reliable pseudo supervision, enabling scalable semi-supervised medical image segmentation under limited annotations.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses issues in semi-supervised breast ultrasound image segmentation by introducing a novel framework combining vision-language models with training-free pseudo-label generation and selective label refinement, achieving strong results with minimal labels.
AI.motivation: To overcome unstable pseudo-labels and limited effectiveness of vision-language models in breast ultrasound image segmentation under semi-supervised settings.
AI.method: The framework employs descriptive cross-domain prompts for pseudo-label generation, a static teacher to utilize global priors, and techniques like uncertainty-weighted fusion and reverse contrastive learning to improve performance.
AI.result: The method achieves results comparable to fully supervised models, even with only 2.5% labeled data, and outperforms existing SSL methods across four datasets.
AI.conclusion: The framework is an effective and scalable solution for semi-supervised medical image segmentation, easily adaptable to different imaging modalities or diseases.

title: JOPP-3D: Joint Open Vocabulary Semantic Segmentation on Point Clouds and Panoramas
summary: Semantic segmentation across visual modalities such as 3D point clouds and panoramic images remains a challenging task, primarily due to the scarcity of annotated data and the limited adaptability of fixed-label models. In this paper, we present JOPP-3D, an open-vocabulary semantic segmentation framework that jointly leverages panoramic and point cloud data to enable language-driven scene understanding. We convert RGB-D panoramic images into their corresponding tangential perspective images and 3D point clouds, then use these modalities to extract and align foundational vision-language features. This allows natural language querying to generate semantic masks on both input modalities. Experimental evaluation on the Stanford-2D-3D-s and ToF-360 datasets demonstrates the capability of JOPP-3D to produce coherent and semantically meaningful segmentations across panoramic and 3D domains. Our proposed method achieves a significant improvement compared to the SOTA in open and closed vocabulary 2D and 3D semantic segmentation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces JOPP-3D, a framework for open-vocabulary semantic segmentation that combines panoramic images and 3D point clouds for language-driven scene understanding.
AI.motivation: To address the challenges of annotated data scarcity and adaptability of fixed-label models in semantic segmentation tasks across modalities like panoramic images and 3D point clouds.
AI.method: JOPP-3D converts RGB-D panoramic images into tangential perspective and 3D point clouds, aligns foundational vision-language features, and enables natural language queries for semantic segmentation.
AI.result: Experiments on Stanford-2D-3D-s and ToF-360 datasets show JOPP-3D's ability to produce high-quality semantic segmentations, outperforming state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: JOPP-3D proves effective at enabling cross-modal semantic segmentation with language queries, setting a new benchmark for open and closed vocabulary segmentation.

title: Optimizing 3D Diffusion Models for Medical Imaging via Multi-Scale Reward Learning
summary: Diffusion models have emerged as powerful tools for 3D medical image generation, yet bridging the gap between standard training objectives and clinical relevance remains a challenge. This paper presents a method to enhance 3D diffusion models using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with multi-scale feedback. We first pretrain a 3D diffusion model on MRI volumes to establish a robust generative prior. Subsequently, we fine-tune the model using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO), guided by a novel reward system that integrates both 2D slice-wise assessments and 3D volumetric analysis. This combination allows the model to simultaneously optimize for local texture details and global structural coherence. We validate our framework on the BraTS 2019 and OASIS-1 datasets. Our results indicate that incorporating RL feedback effectively steers the generation process toward higher quality distributions. Quantitative analysis reveals significant improvements in Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and, crucially, the synthetic data demonstrates enhanced utility in downstream tumor and disease classification tasks compared to non-optimized baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a novel approach using Reinforcement Learning (RL) to improve 3D diffusion models for medical image generation.
AI.motivation: To enhance the clinical relevance of 3D medical image generation by addressing limitations in standard diffusion model objectives.
AI.method: The authors pretrain a 3D diffusion model on MRI data, then fine-tune it using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO) and a multi-scale RL feedback system that balances 2D texture and 3D structure.
AI.result: The proposed method significantly improves metrics like Fréchet Inception Distance (FID) and enhances the synthetic data's ability to support downstream classification tasks.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating multi-scale RL feedback into diffusion models results in higher-quality medical images with improved clinical utility.

title: Making Training-Free Diffusion Segmentors Scale with the Generative Power
summary: As powerful generative models, text-to-image diffusion models have recently been explored for discriminative tasks. A line of research focuses on adapting a pre-trained diffusion model to semantic segmentation without any further training, leading to what training-free diffusion segmentors. These methods typically rely on cross-attention maps from the model's attention layers, which are assumed to capture semantic relationships between image pixels and text tokens. Ideally, such approaches should benefit from more powerful diffusion models, i.e., stronger generative capability should lead to better segmentation. However, we observe that existing methods often fail to scale accordingly. To understand this issue, we identify two underlying gaps: (i) cross-attention is computed across multiple heads and layers, but there exists a discrepancy between these individual attention maps and a unified global representation. (ii) Even when a global map is available, it does not directly translate to accurate semantic correlation for segmentation, due to score imbalances among different text tokens. To bridge these gaps, we propose two techniques: auto aggregation and per-pixel rescaling, which together enable training-free segmentation to better leverage generative capability. We evaluate our approach on standard semantic segmentation benchmarks and further integrate it into a generative technique, demonstrating both improved performance broad applicability. Codes are at https://github.com/Darkbblue/goca.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes enhanced training-free segmentation techniques to better utilize text-to-image diffusion models for semantic segmentation using auto aggregation and per-pixel rescaling.
AI.motivation: To address the issue of improving segmentation results by leveraging more powerful generative text-to-image diffusion models without further training.
AI.method: Two techniques are introduced: (1) Auto aggregation to unify cross-attention maps for global representation. (2) Per-pixel rescaling to adjust score imbalances among text tokens.
AI.result: Improved performance in semantic segmentation benchmarks, showing enhanced segmentation capabilities using generative models without training.
AI.conclusion: Enhanced segmentation methods bridge existing gaps in cross-attention representation, enabling generative models to perform better in semantic segmentation without additional training.

title: The Coordination Gap: Alternation Metrics for Temporal Dynamics in Multi-Agent Battle of the Exes
comment: 40 pages, 5 figures, 4 tables. Submitted to Mathematical Social Sciences
summary:
Multi-agent coordination dilemmas expose a fundamental tension between individual optimization and collective welfare, yet characterizing such coordination requires metrics sensitive to temporal structure and collective dynamics. As a diagnostic testbed, we study a BoE-derived multi-agent variant of the Battle of the Exes, formalizing it as a Markov game in which turn-taking emerges as a periodic coordination regime. Conventional outcome-based metrics (e.g., efficiency and min/max fairness) are temporally blind -- they cannot distinguish structured alternation from monopolistic or random access patterns -- and fairness ratios lose discriminative power as n grows, obscuring inequities.
  To address this limitation, we introduce Perfect Alternation (PA) as a reference coordination regime and propose six novel Alternation (ALT) metrics designed as temporally sensitive observables of coordination quality. Using Q-learning agents as a minimal adaptive diagnostic baseline, and comparing against random-policy null processes, we uncover a clear measurement failure: despite exhibiting deceptively high traditional metrics (e.g., reward fairness often exceeding 0.9), learned policies perform up to 81% below random baselines under ALT-variant evaluation -- a deficit already present in the two-agent case and intensifying as n grows.
  These results demonstrate, in this setting, that high aggregate payoffs can coexist with poor temporal coordination, and that conventional metrics may severely mischaracterize emergent dynamics. Our findings underscore the necessity of temporally aware observables for analyzing coordination in multi-agent games and highlight random-policy baselines as essential null processes for interpreting coordination outcomes relative to chance-level behavior.

AI.tldr: This paper studies coordination in multi-agent settings, introducing temporally sensitive metrics to better assess coordination quality and uncovering key limitations in conventional metrics.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses the inability of traditional metrics to accurately evaluate dynamics and fairness in multi-agent coordination, particularly in temporal settings.
AI.method: The authors formalize a multi-agent testbed based on the Battle of the Exes game and define six novel Alternation (ALT) metrics complemented by a Perfect Alternation reference regime. Q-learning agents and random-policy baselines are employed for evaluation.
AI.result: The findings reveal that traditional metrics (e.g., fairness ratios) misrepresent coordination quality, often showing high scores despite significant temporal coordination deficits, as highlighted under ALT metrics.
AI.conclusion: The results indicate the importance of temporally aware metrics and random-policy baselines in accurately characterizing coordination in multi-agent settings, showcasing the pitfalls of current evaluation techniques.

title: Sensitivity-Aware Retrieval-Augmented Intent Clarification
comment: Accepted to CoSCIN@ECIR2026 (Workshop on Conversational Search for Complex Information Needs)
summary: In conversational search systems, a key component is to determine and clarify the intent behind complex queries. We view intent clarification in light of the exploratory search paradigm, where users, through an iterative, evolving process of selection, exploration and retrieval, transform a visceral or conscious need into a formalized one. Augmenting the clarification component with a retrieval step (retrieval-augmented intent clarification) can seriously enhance clarification performance, especially in domains where Large Language Models (LLMs) lack parametric knowledge. However, in more sensitive domains, such as healthcare, government (e.g. FOIA search) or legal contexts, the retrieval database may contain sensitive information that needs protection. In this paper, we explore the research challenge of developing a retrieval-augmented conversational agent that can act as a mediator and gatekeeper for the sensitive collection. To do that, we also need to know what we are protecting and against what. We propose to tackle this research challenge in three steps: 1) define an attack model, 2) design sensitivity-aware defenses on the retrieval level and 3) develop evaluation methods to measure the trade-off between the level of protection and the system's utility.

AI.tldr: The paper focuses on improving intent clarification in conversational search, particularly in sensitive domains, by introducing a retrieval-augmented approach while considering data protection.
AI.motivation: To address challenges in determining and clarifying user search intent, especially in sensitive domains, where existing methods may not adequately protect sensitive information.
AI.method: The authors propose a three-step approach: defining an attack model, designing sensitivity-aware defenses at the retrieval level, and creating evaluation methods to balance protection with system utility.
AI.result: The paper highlights the research groundwork for developing conversational agents that mediate sensitive data while maintaining effective retrieval and clarification capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The work emphasizes the importance of balancing data protection with system performance in conversational search, paving the way for secure and utility-focused solutions.

title: Towards Motion Turing Test: Evaluating Human-Likeness in Humanoid Robots
summary: Humanoid robots have achieved significant progress in motion generation and control, exhibiting movements that appear increasingly natural and human-like. Inspired by the Turing Test, we propose the Motion Turing Test, a framework that evaluates whether human observers can discriminate between humanoid robot and human poses using only kinematic information. To facilitate this evaluation, we present the Human-Humanoid Motion (HHMotion) dataset, which consists of 1,000 motion sequences spanning 15 action categories, performed by 11 humanoid models and 10 human subjects. All motion sequences are converted into SMPL-X representations to eliminate the influence of visual appearance. We recruited 30 annotators to rate the human-likeness of each pose on a 0-5 scale, resulting in over 500 hours of annotation. Analysis of the collected data reveals that humanoid motions still exhibit noticeable deviations from human movements, particularly in dynamic actions such as jumping, boxing, and running. Building on HHMotion, we formulate a human-likeness evaluation task that aims to automatically predict human-likeness scores from motion data. Despite recent progress in multimodal large language models, we find that they remain inadequate for assessing motion human-likeness. To address this, we propose a simple baseline model and demonstrate that it outperforms several contemporary LLM-based methods. The dataset, code, and benchmark will be publicly released to support future research in the community.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces the Motion Turing Test to assess whether observers can distinguish humanoid robot motions from human motions based on kinematic data using the new HHMotion dataset. The paper finds that humanoid motions still differ from human motions, especially in dynamic actions, and proposes a baseline evaluation model outperforming recent methods.
AI.motivation: The authors were motivated to assess the human-likeness of humanoid robot motions and identify distinctions from human movements for improving humanoid motion generation and control.
AI.method: They developed the Motion Turing Test framework and created the HHMotion dataset comprising 1,000 motion sequences in SMPL-X format. Human annotators rated the human-likeness of these poses, and a baseline evaluation model was proposed to predict scores.
AI.result: Analysis shows humanoid robot movements deviate from human movements, particularly in dynamic actions. The proposed baseline evaluation model surpasses several multimodal large language models in predicting human-likeness scores.
AI.conclusion: Humanoid robots require further refinement to achieve human-like motion, especially in dynamic activities. The released dataset, evaluation framework, and findings can guide future research in this field.

title: SpaCRD: Multimodal Deep Fusion of Histology and Spatial Transcriptomics for Cancer Region Detection
summary: Accurate detection of cancer tissue regions (CTR) enables deeper analysis of the tumor microenvironment and offers crucial insights into treatment response. Traditional CTR detection methods, which typically rely on the rich cellular morphology in histology images, are susceptible to a high rate of false positives due to morphological similarities across different tissue regions. The groundbreaking advances in spatial transcriptomics (ST) provide detailed cellular phenotypes and spatial localization information, offering new opportunities for more accurate cancer region detection. However, current methods are unable to effectively integrate histology images with ST data, especially in the context of cross-sample and cross-platform/batch settings for accomplishing the CTR detection. To address this challenge, we propose SpaCRD, a transfer learning-based method that deeply integrates histology images and ST data to enable reliable CTR detection across diverse samples, platforms, and batches. Once trained on source data, SpaCRD can be readily generalized to accurately detect cancerous regions across samples from different platforms and batches. The core of SpaCRD is a category-regularized variational reconstruction-guided bidirectional cross-attention fusion network, which enables the model to adaptively capture latent co-expression patterns between histological features and gene expression from multiple perspectives. Extensive benchmark analysis on 23 matched histology-ST datasets spanning various disease types, platforms, and batches demonstrates that SpaCRD consistently outperforms existing eight state-of-the-art methods in CTR detection.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SpaCRD, a transfer learning-based model for accurate cancer tissue region (CTR) detection by integrating histology images and spatial transcriptomics (ST) data, even across different samples, platforms, and batches. It outperforms eight existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current cancer tissue region (CTR) detection methods face high false positive rates due to morphological similarities in histology images. Furthermore, the inability to effectively integrate histology images with spatial transcriptomics (ST) data in diverse and cross-platform settings motivates this study.
AI.method: The proposed SpaCRD integrates histology images and ST data using a category-regularized variational reconstruction-guided bidirectional cross-attention fusion network. It employs transfer learning to generalize CTR detection across varying samples, platforms, and batches.
AI.result: SpaCRD demonstrates superior performance in CTR detection, outperforming eight state-of-the-art methods across 23 matched histology-ST datasets covering various diseases and platforms.
AI.conclusion: SpaCRD offers a robust and reliable approach to CTR detection by leveraging integrated histology and ST data, achieving high accuracy and generalizability across diverse datasets and platforms.

title: Adaptive Language-Aware Image Reflection Removal Network
summary: Existing image reflection removal methods struggle to handle complex reflections. Accurate language descriptions can help the model understand the image content to remove complex reflections. However, due to blurred and distorted interferences in reflected images, machine-generated language descriptions of the image content are often inaccurate, which harms the performance of language-guided reflection removal. To address this, we propose the Adaptive Language-Aware Network (ALANet) to remove reflections even with inaccurate language inputs. Specifically, ALANet integrates both filtering and optimization strategies. The filtering strategy reduces the negative effects of language while preserving its benefits, whereas the optimization strategy enhances the alignment between language and visual features. ALANet also utilizes language cues to decouple specific layer content from feature maps, improving its ability to handle complex reflections. To evaluate the model's performance under complex reflections and varying levels of language accuracy, we introduce the Complex Reflection and Language Accuracy Variance (CRLAV) dataset. Experimental results demonstrate that ALANet surpasses state-of-the-art methods for image reflection removal. The code and dataset are available at https://github.com/fashyon/ALANet.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ALANet, a model for image reflection removal that integrates language guidance even when text descriptions are inaccurate. It outperforms existing methods.
AI.motivation: Existing methods struggle with complex reflections, and machine-generated descriptions of images can be inaccurate due to interference. This limits effective language-aided reflection removal, which this paper aims to improve.
AI.method: ALANet integrates filtering and optimization strategies to manage inaccurate language descriptions. Filtering minimizes harmful language effects and optimization aligns language and visual features. Additionally, ALANet decouples layers in feature maps using language cues.
AI.result: ALANet outperformed state-of-the-art reflection removal methods, validated on the newly introduced CRLAV dataset.
AI.conclusion: ALANet proves to be an effective solution for reflection removal with complex reflections, even when language guidance is imprecise.

title: Point-Supervised Skeleton-Based Human Action Segmentation
summary: Skeleton-based temporal action segmentation is a fundamental yet challenging task, playing a crucial role in enabling intelligent systems to perceive and respond to human activities. While fully-supervised methods achieve satisfactory performance, they require costly frame-level annotations and are sensitive to ambiguous action boundaries. To address these issues, we introduce a point-supervised framework for skeleton-based action segmentation, where only a single frame per action segment is labeled. We leverage multimodal skeleton data, including joint, bone, and motion information, encoded via a pretrained unified model to extract rich feature representations. To generate reliable pseudo-labels, we propose a novel prototype similarity method and integrate it with two existing methods: energy function and constrained K-Medoids clustering. Multimodal pseudo-label integration is proposed to enhance the reliability of the pseudo-label and guide the model training. We establish new benchmarks on PKU-MMD (X-Sub and X-View), MCFS-22, and MCFS-130, and implement baselines for point-supervised skeleton-based human action segmentation. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves competitive performance, even surpassing some fully-supervised methods while significantly reducing annotation effort.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a point-supervised framework for skeleton-based action segmentation, minimizing annotation effort by labeling only one frame per action segment while achieving competitive performance.
AI.motivation: Current fully-supervised methods for skeleton-based temporal action segmentation require costly frame-level annotations and struggle with ambiguous action boundaries. The paper aims to reduce annotation requirements and improve segmentation reliability.
AI.method: The method relies on multimodal skeleton data (joint, bone, motion), using a pretrained unified model for feature extraction. A prototype similarity method integrates with two existing methods (energy function and constrained K-Medoids clustering) to generate pseudo-labels. Multimodal pseudo-label integration further enhances training.
AI.result: The proposed method achieves competitive performance, outperforming some fully-supervised approaches across benchmarks like PKU-MMD (X-Sub, X-View), MCFS-22, and MCFS-130, while requiring significantly fewer annotations.
AI.conclusion: The framework successfully reduces annotation efforts for skeleton-based action segmentation, leveraging multimodal data and pseudo-labeling techniques to maintain or surpass fully-supervised method performance.

title: Cut to the Chase: Training-free Multimodal Summarization via Chain-of-Events
summary: Multimodal Summarization (MMS) aims to generate concise textual summaries by understanding and integrating information across videos, transcripts, and images. However, existing approaches still suffer from three main challenges: (1) reliance on domain-specific supervision, (2) implicit fusion with weak cross-modal grounding, and (3) flat temporal modeling without event transitions. To address these issues, we introduce **CoE**, a training-free MMS framework that performs structured reasoning through a **Chain-of-Events** guided by a Hierarchical Event Graph (HEG). The HEG encodes textual semantics into an explicit event hierarchy that scaffolds cross-modal grounding and temporal reasoning. Guided by this structure, **CoE** localizes key visual cues, models event evolution and causal transitions, and refines outputs via lightweight style adaptation for domain alignment. Extensive experiments on eight diverse datasets demonstrate that **CoE** consistently outperforms state-of-the-art video CoT baselines, achieving average gains of **+3.04 ROUGE**, **+9.51 CIDEr**, and **+1.88 BERTScore**, highlighting its robustness, interpretability, and cross-domain generalization. Our code is available at https://github.com/youxiaoxing/CoE.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CoE, a training-free multimodal summarization framework using structured reasoning with a Hierarchical Event Graph.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches in multimodal summarization face challenges such as domain-specific reliance, weak cross-modal grounding, and inadequate temporal event modeling.
AI.method: The CoE framework employs a Hierarchical Event Graph to encode textual semantics into an event hierarchy for structured reasoning, enabling cross-modal integration, event modeling, and style adaptations.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on eight datasets show CoE's consistent improvement over baselines with enhancements in ROUGE (+3.04), CIDEr (+9.51), and BERTScore (+1.88).
AI.conclusion: CoE offers robustness, interpretability, and cross-domain generalization in multimodal summarization, using its training-free and event-centric approach.

title: StreamVoiceAnon+: Emotion-Preserving Streaming Speaker Anonymization via Frame-Level Acoustic Distillation
summary: We address the challenge of preserving emotional content in streaming speaker anonymization (SA). Neural audio codec language models trained for audio continuation tend to degrade source emotion: content tokens discard emotional information, and the model defaults to dominant acoustic patterns rather than preserving paralinguistic attributes. We propose supervised finetuning with neutral-emotion utterance pairs from the same speaker, combined with frame-level emotion distillation on acoustic token hidden states. All modifications are confined to finetuning, which takes less than 2 hours on 4 GPUs and adds zero inference latency overhead, while maintaining a competitive 180ms streaming latency. On the VoicePrivacy 2024 protocol, our approach achieves a 49.2% UAR (emotion preservation) with 5.77% WER (intelligibility), a +24% relative UAR improvement over the baseline (39.7%->49.2%) and +10% over the emotion-prompt variant (44.6% UAR), while maintaining strong privacy (EER 49.0%). Demo and code are available: https://anonymous3842031239.github.io/

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the challenge of preserving emotions in streaming speaker anonymization (SA) by leveraging supervised fine-tuning and emotion distillation methods, offering significant improvements in emotional preservation, intelligibility, and privacy.
AI.motivation: Preserving emotional content while anonymizing speakers in real-time is challenging, as current systems discard emotional nuances or default to dominant patterns, negatively affecting user experience.
AI.method: Proposed supervised fine-tuning using neutral-emotion utterance pairs and frame-level emotion distillation on acoustic token hidden states. This involves less than 2 hours of fine-tuning and incurs no extra inference latency.
AI.result: Demonstrated a 49.2% UAR (emotion preservation), 5.77% WER (intelligibility), and robust privacy (EER 49.0%) on the VoicePrivacy 2024 protocol, outperforming both baselines and emotion-prompt methods.
AI.conclusion: The approach effectively preserves emotions in streaming speaker anonymization, achieving significant improvements in emotional retention, intelligibility, and maintaining privacy.

title: EntON: Eigenentropy-Optimized Neighborhood Densification in 3D Gaussian Splatting
comment: Submitted to ISPRS Journal of Photogrammetry and Remote Sensing on 20 February 2026
summary: We present a novel Eigenentropy-optimized neighboorhood densification strategy EntON in 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS) for geometrically accurate and high-quality rendered 3D reconstruction. While standard 3DGS produces Gaussians whose centers and surfaces are poorly aligned with the underlying object geometry, surface-focused reconstruction methods frequently sacrifice photometric accuracy. In contrast to the conventional densification strategy, which relies on the magnitude of the view-space position gradient, our approach introduces a geometry-aware strategy to guide adaptive splitting and pruning. Specifically, we compute the 3D shape feature Eigenentropy from the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix in the k-nearest neighborhood of each Gaussian center, which quantifies the local structural order. These Eigenentropy values are integrated into an alternating optimization framework: During the optimization process, the algorithm alternates between (i) standard gradient-based densification, which refines regions via view-space gradients, and (ii) Eigenentropy-aware densification, which preferentially densifies Gaussians in low-Eigenentropy (ordered, flat) neighborhoods to better capture fine geometric details on the object surface, and prunes those in high-Eigenentropy (disordered, spherical) regions. We provide quantitative and qualitative evaluations on two benchmark datasets: small-scale DTU dataset and large-scale TUM2TWIN dataset, covering man-made objects and urban scenes. Experiments demonstrate that our Eigenentropy-aware alternating densification strategy improves geometric accuracy by up to 33% and rendering quality by up to 7%, while reducing the number of Gaussians by up to 50% and training time by up to 23%. Overall, EnTON achieves a favorable balance between geometric accuracy, rendering quality and efficiency by avoiding unnecessary scene expansion.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces EntON, an optimized neighborhood densification strategy for 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS), enhancing geometric accuracy and rendering quality while reducing computational demands.
AI.motivation: Current 3D reconstruction methods using 3DGS have limitations in aligning Gaussians accurately with the object geometry, and existing surface-focused approaches compromise photometric accuracy.
AI.method: The EntON strategy leverages Eigenentropy, calculated from covariance matrices' eigenvalues, to guide adaptive splitting and pruning of Gaussians. It alternates between view-space gradient optimization and Eigenentropy-based densification.
AI.result: Experiments on benchmark datasets show improved geometric accuracy by up to 33%, rendering quality by 7%, reduced Gaussian count by 50%, and decreased training time by 23%.
AI.conclusion: EntON method achieves a better balance of geometric accuracy, rendering quality, and computational efficiency, making it suitable for high-quality 3D reconstruction.

title: Word-Anchored Temporal Forgery Localization
summary: Current temporal forgery localization (TFL) approaches typically rely on temporal boundary regression or continuous frame-level anomaly detection paradigms to derive candidate forgery proposals. However, they suffer not only from feature granularity misalignment but also from costly computation. To address these issues, we propose word-anchored temporal forgery localization (WAFL), a novel paradigm that shifts the TFL task from temporal regression and continuous localization to discrete word-level binary classification. Specifically, we first analyze the essence of temporal forgeries and identify the minimum meaningful forgery units, word tokens, and then align data preprocessing with the natural linguistic boundaries of speech. To adapt powerful pre-trained foundation backbones for feature extraction, we introduce the forensic feature realignment (FFR) module, mapping representations from the pre-trained semantic space to a discriminative forensic manifold. This allows subsequent lightweight linear classifiers to efficiently perform binary classification and accomplish the TFL task. Furthermore, to overcome the extreme class imbalance inherent to forgery detection, we design the artifact-centric asymmetric (ACA) loss, which breaks the standard precision-recall trade-off by dynamically suppressing overwhelming authentic gradients while asymmetrically prioritizing subtle forensic artifacts. Extensive experiments demonstrate that WAFL significantly outperforms state-of-the-art approaches in localization performance under both in- and cross-dataset settings, while requiring substantially fewer learnable parameters and operating at high computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces WAFL, a novel and efficient method for temporal forgery localization by shifting focus to discrete word-level classification instead of traditional approaches, achieving superior performance and reduced computational requirements.
AI.motivation: Current approaches for temporal forgery localization face challenges such as feature misalignment and high computational cost, necessitating a more efficient and accurate method.
AI.method: WAFL leverages word-level binary classification, paired with forensic feature realignment (FFR) for better feature extraction and ACA loss to address class imbalance and enhance precision for subtle forensic artifacts.
AI.result: WAFL outperforms state-of-the-art TFL methods in localization accuracy across various datasets, while being computationally efficient and using fewer model parameters.
AI.conclusion: WAFL offers a paradigm shift in temporal forgery localization, significantly improving performance while maintaining high efficiency, addressing key challenges in the field.

title: Low-latency Event-based Object Detection with Spatially-Sparse Linear Attention
summary: Event cameras provide sequential visual data with spatial sparsity and high temporal resolution, making them attractive for low-latency object detection. Existing asynchronous event-based neural networks realize this low-latency advantage by updating predictions event-by-event, but still suffer from two bottlenecks: recurrent architectures are difficult to train efficiently on long sequences, and improving accuracy often increases per-event computation and latency. Linear attention is appealing in this setting because it supports parallel training and recurrent inference. However, standard linear attention updates a global state for every event, yielding a poor accuracy-efficiency trade-off, which is problematic for object detection, where fine-grained representations and thus states are preferred. The key challenge is therefore to introduce sparse state activation that exploits event sparsity while preserving efficient parallel training. We propose Spatially-Sparse Linear Attention (SSLA), which introduces a mixture-of-spaces state decomposition and a scatter-compute-gather training procedure, enabling state-level sparsity as well as training parallelism. Built on SSLA, we develop an end-to-end asynchronous linear attention model, SSLA-Det, for event-based object detection. On Gen1 and N-Caltech101, SSLA-Det achieves state-of-the-art accuracy among asynchronous methods, reaching 0.375 mAP and 0.515 mAP, respectively, while reducing per-event computation by more than 20 times compared to the strongest prior asynchronous baseline, demonstrating the potential of linear attention for low-latency event-based vision.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a novel model, SSLA-Det, for low-latency event-based object detection using Spatially-Sparse Linear Attention to optimize accuracy and efficiency.
AI.motivation: Existing asynchronous event-based neural networks are limited by inefficient training for long sequences and increased latency when accuracy is improved, necessitating an innovation that maintains training parallelism and event-level processing efficiency.
AI.method: The paper proposes SSLA: Spatially-Sparse Linear Attention with a mixture-of-spaces state decomposition and a scatter-compute-gather training process to capitalize on event sparsity while maintaining efficient training.
AI.result: SSLA-Det achieved state-of-the-art accuracy for asynchronous methods on benchmarks (Gen1: 0.375 mAP, N-Caltech101: 0.515 mAP) and reduced per-event computation by over 20 times compared to prior baselines.
AI.conclusion: The study demonstrates that linear attention can significantly enhance the efficiency and performance of asynchronous event-based object detection systems.

title: Hierarchical Collaborative Fusion for 3D Instance-aware Referring Expression Segmentation
summary: Generalised 3D Referring Expression Segmentation (3D-GRES) localizes objects in 3D scenes based on natural language, even when descriptions match multiple or zero targets. Existing methods rely solely on sparse point clouds, lacking rich visual semantics for fine-grained descriptions. We propose HCF-RES, a multi-modal framework with two key innovations. First, Hierarchical Visual Semantic Decomposition leverages SAM instance masks to guide CLIP encoding at dual granularities -- pixel-level and instance-level features -- preserving object boundaries during 2D-to-3D projection. Second, Progressive Multi-level Fusion integrates representations through intra-modal collaboration, cross-modal adaptive weighting between 2D semantic and 3D geometric features, and language-guided refinement. HCF-RES achieves state-of-the-art results on both ScanRefer and Multi3DRefer.

AI.tldr: HCF-RES introduces a multimodal framework for 3D Referring Expression Segmentation, combining advanced visual semantic decomposition and multi-level fusion strategies to achieve state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the limitations of existing 3D-GRES techniques, which rely solely on sparse point clouds and lack rich visual semantics for dealing with fine-grained natural language descriptions.
AI.method: HCF-RES incorporates Hierarchical Visual Semantic Decomposition (to extract pixel- and instance-level features by leveraging SAM instance masks and CLIP encoding) and Progressive Multi-level Fusion (combining representations via intra-modal collaboration, adaptive cross-modal weighting, and language-guided refinement).
AI.result: HCF-RES demonstrates state-of-the-art results on the ScanRefer and Multi3DRefer benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The proposed HCF-RES framework significantly enhances 3D object localization capabilities by integrating multimodal features effectively, paving the way for more accurate and detailed scene understanding.

title: Efficient Vector Search in the Wild: One Model for Multi-K Queries
summary: Learned top-K search is a promising approach for serving vector queries with both high accuracy and performance. However, current models trained for a specific K value fail to generalize to real-world multi-K queries: they suffer from accuracy degradation (for larger Ks) and performance loss (for smaller Ks). Training the model to generalize on different Ks requires orders of magnitude more preprocessing time and is not suitable for serving vector queries in the wild. We present OMEGA, a K-generalizable learned top-K search method that simultaneously achieves high accuracy, high performance, and low preprocessing cost for multi-K vector queries. The key idea is that a base model properly trained on K=1 with our trajectory-based features can be used to accurately predict larger Ks with a dynamic refinement procedure and smaller Ks with minimal performance loss. To make our refinements efficient, we further leverage the statistical properties of top-K searches to reduce excessive model invocations. Extensive evaluations on multiple public and production datasets show that, under the same preprocessing budgets, OMEGA achieves 6-33% lower average latency compared to state-of-the-art learned search methods, while all systems achieve the same recall target. With only 16-30% of the preprocessing time, OMEGA attains 1.01-1.28x of the optimal average latency of these baselines.

AI.tldr: OMEGA is introduced as a learned top-K vector search method, specifically designed to handle multi-K queries efficiently, addressing limitations of existing methods.
AI.motivation: Current learned vector search methods trained for specific K values suffer from performance and accuracy issues with varying K values, necessitating a generalizable and efficient solution.
AI.method: OMEGA leverages trajectory-based features trained on K=1, combined with dynamic refinement techniques, and exploits statistical properties of top-K searches to optimize query resolution without excessive model invocation.
AI.result: OMEGA delivers up to 33% lower latency compared to current methods, achieving efficient recall and reducing preprocessing costs to 16-30% of competitors, while maintaining strong performance across multiple datasets.
AI.conclusion: OMEGA successfully generalizes across multi-K vector queries with minimal preprocessing time and achieves superior latency and accuracy performance compared to existing learned search methods.

title: GazeMoE: Perception of Gaze Target with Mixture-of-Experts
summary: Estimating human gaze target from visible images is a critical task for robots to understand human attention, yet the development of generalizable neural architectures and training paradigms remains challenging. While recent advances in pre-trained vision foundation models offer promising avenues for locating gaze targets, the integration of multi-modal cues -- including eyes, head poses, gestures, and contextual features -- demands adaptive and efficient decoding mechanisms. Inspired by Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) for adaptive domain expertise in large vision-language models, we propose GazeMoE, a novel end-to-end framework that selectively leverages gaze-target-related cues from a frozen foundation model through MoE modules. To address class imbalance in gaze target classification (in-frame vs. out-of-frame) and enhance robustness, GazeMoE incorporates a class-balancing auxiliary loss alongside strategic data augmentations, including region-specific cropping and photometric transformations. Extensive experiments on benchmark datasets demonstrate that our GazeMoE achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming existing methods on challenging gaze estimation tasks. The code and pre-trained models are released at https://huggingface.co/zdai257/GazeMoE

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GazeMoE, an adaptive framework using Mixture-of-Experts for robust human gaze target estimation, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of estimating human gaze targets by integrating multi-modal cues in a generalizable framework.
AI.method: GazeMoE uses a Mixture-of-Experts mechanism with a frozen foundation model, auxiliary loss for class balancing, and strategic data augmentation.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate superior performance of GazeMoE compared to existing gaze estimation methods on benchmark datasets.
AI.conclusion: GazeMoE successfully integrates multi-modal cues and adaptive mechanisms to advance gaze estimation tasks, providing state-of-the-art results and making public code available for further research.

title: ODD-SEC: Onboard Drone Detection with a Spinning Event Camera
summary: The rapid proliferation of drones requires balancing innovation with regulation.
