In this work, we propose a lightweight and interpretable framework for detecting motion related artifacts in T1 weighted brain MRI by extending the Discriminative Histogram of Gradient Magnitude (DHoGM) to a three dimensional space. The proposed method integrates complementary slice-level (2D) and volume-level (3D) DHoGM features through a parallel decision strategy, capturing both localized and global motion-induced degradation. Volumetric analysis is performed using overlapping 3D cuboids to achieve comprehensive spatial coverage while maintaining computational efficiency. A simple threshold-based classifier and a low parameter multilayer perceptron are used, which results in a model with only 209 trainable parameters. Our method was evaluated on the MR-ART and ABIDE datasets under both seen-site and unseen-site conditions. Experimental results demonstrate strong performance, achieving up to 94.34\% accuracy the in domain evaluation and 89\% accuracy on unseen sites, while almost completely avoiding false acceptance of poor-quality scans. Ablation studies confirms the complementary benefits of combining 2D and 3D features. Overall, the proposed approach offers an effective, efficient, and robust solution for automated MRI quality check, with strong potential for integration into large scale clinical and research workflows.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces an efficient and interpretable method for detecting motion-related artifacts in structural brain MRI by leveraging enhanced 3D Discriminative Histogram of Gradient Magnitude (DHoGM) features, achieving strong performance and generalization.
AI.motivation: The paper aims to address challenges in motion artifacts and poor generalization in automated quality assessment of structural brain MRI, which are critical for reliable neuroimaging analysis.
AI.method: The authors extend the Discriminative Histogram of Gradient Magnitude (DHoGM) to 3D space, integrate 2D and 3D features via a parallel decision strategy, and utilize computationally efficient classifiers like threshold-based models and low-parameter MLPs.
AI.result: The approach demonstrated excellent performance on both seen and unseen site datasets (MR-ART and ABIDE), achieving up to 94.34% accuracy in-domain and 89% accuracy unseen-site evaluation, with almost no false acceptance of low-quality scans.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method is effective, efficient, and robust, offering strong potential for integration into large-scale clinical and research workflows for automated MRI quality assessments.

title: Uni-LVC: A Unified Method for Intra- and Inter-Mode Learned Video Compression
summary: Recent advances in learned video compression (LVC) have led to significant performance gains, with codecs such as DCVC-RT surpassing the H.266/VVC low-delay mode in compression efficiency. However, existing LVCs still exhibit key limitations: they often require separate models for intra and inter coding modes, and their performance degrades when temporal references are unreliable. To address this, we introduce Uni-LVC, a unified LVC method that supports both intra and inter coding with low-delay and random-access in a single model. Building on a strong intra-codec, Uni-LVC formulates inter-coding as intra-coding conditioned on temporal information extracted from reference frames. We design an efficient cross-attention adaptation module that integrates temporal cues, enabling seamless support for both unidirectional (low-delay) and bidirectional (random-access) prediction modes. A reliability-aware classifier is proposed to selectively scale the temporal cues, making Uni-LVC behave closer to intra coding when references are unreliable. We further propose a multistage training strategy to facilitate adaptive learning across various coding modes. Extensive experiments demonstrate that Uni-LVC achieves superior rate-distortion performance in intra and inter configurations while maintaining comparable computational efficiency.

AI.tldr: Uni-LVC proposes a unified learned video compression method effective in both intra and inter coding modes, improving rate-distortion performance and computational efficiency.
AI.motivation: To overcome the limitations of existing learned video compression methods, such as requiring separate models for intra and inter coding modes and performance drops due to unreliable temporal references.
AI.method: Uni-LVC leverages a unified model integrating temporal information from reference frames using an efficient cross-attention module, a reliability-aware classifier for adapting to changing temporal reference conditions, and a multistage training strategy.
AI.result: The proposed model demonstrates superior rate-distortion performance in both coding configurations without compromising computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Uni-LVC successfully addresses key limitations of learned video compression approaches, providing an effective and efficient solution for unified intra and inter coding.

title: Architectural Unification for Polarimetric Imaging Across Multiple Degradations
summary: Polarimetric imaging aims to recover polarimetric parameters, including Total Intensity (TI), Degree of Polarization (DoP), and Angle of Polarization (AoP), from captured polarized measurements. In real-world scenarios, these measurements are frequently affected by diverse degradations such as low-light noise, motion blur, and mosaicing artifacts. Due to the nonlinear dependency of DoP and AoP on the measured intensities, accurately retrieving physically consistent polarimetric parameters from degraded observations remains highly challenging. Existing approaches typically adopt task-specific network architectures tailored to individual degradation types, limiting their adaptability across different restoration scenarios. Moreover, many methods rely on multi-stage processing pipelines that suffer from error accumulation, or operate solely in a single domain (either image or Stokes domain), failing to fully exploit the intrinsic physical relationships between them. In this work, we propose a unified architectural framework for polarimetric imaging that is structurally shared across multiple degradation scenarios. Rather than redesigning network structures for each task, our framework maintains a consistent architectural design while being trained separately for different degradations. The model performs single-stage joint image-Stokes processing, avoiding error accumulation and explicitly preserving physical consistency. Extensive experiments show that this unified architectural design, when trained for specific degradation types, consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across low-light denoising, motion deblurring, and demosaicing tasks, establishing a versatile and physically grounded solution for degraded polarimetric imaging.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a unified framework for polarimetric imaging addressing diverse degradations by jointly processing image-Stokes domains, achieving state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches to polarimetric imaging struggle with adaptability, error accumulation, and incomplete exploitation of physical relationships between domains.
AI.method: A single-stage unified framework structurally shared across various degradation, trained independently for each type, enabling joint image-Stokes domain processing.
AI.result: Extensive experiments demonstrate state-of-the-art outcomes across multiple restoration tasks such as low-light denoising, motion deblurring, and demosaicing.
AI.conclusion: The proposed framework effectively tackles a broad spectrum of degradations in polarimetric imaging, robustly preserving physical consistency and versatility.

title: Enhancing Neural Video Compression of Static Scenes with Positive-Incentive Noise
summary: Static scene videos, such as surveillance feeds and videotelephony streams, constitute a dominant share of storage consumption and network traffic. However, both traditional standardized codecs and neural video compression (NVC) methods struggle to encode these videos efficiently due to inadequate usage of temporal redundancy and severe distribution gaps between training and test data, respectively. While recent generative compression methods improve perceptual quality, they introduce hallucinated details that are unacceptable in authenticity-critical applications. To overcome these limitations, we propose to incorporate positive-incentive noise into NVC for static scene videos, where short-term temporal changes are reinterpreted as positive-incentive noise to facilitate model finetuning. By disentangling transient variations from the persistent background, structured prior information is internalized in the compression model. During inference, the invariant component requires minimal signaling, thus reducing data transmission while maintaining pixel-level fidelity. Preliminary experiments demonstrate a 73% Bjøntegaard delta (BD) rate saving compared to general NVC models. Our method provides an effective solution to trade computation for bandwidth, enabling robust video transmission under adverse network conditions and economic long-term retention of surveillance footage.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a method to enhance neural video compression for static scene videos by incorporating structured noise, reducing data transmission while maintaining fidelity.
AI.motivation: Standard codecs and current neural video compression struggle with static videos due to poor handling of temporal redundancy and distribution mismatches, and existing generative methods compromise authenticity.
AI.method: Incorporates positive-incentive noise to reinterpret short-term changes, separates background from transient variations, and reduces signaling requirements during inference.
AI.result: Achieved a 73% Bjøntegaard delta (BD) rate saving compared to general neural video compression models.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method effectively saves bandwidth, preserves pixel-level fidelity, and provides robust video transmission under poor network conditions, making it ideal for economic storage and surveillance.
